dialogue.utterance: Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. I see you're having trouble breathing. Can you tell me a little more about how you're feeling?

I've been having trouble breathing for about two weeks now. I was admitted to the hospital for sepsis due to pneumonia. I had some breathing treatments, Lasix, and antibiotics. I felt better for a while, but the symptoms are getting worse again.
So your breathing difficulty started around two weeks ago, and now it's getting harder to catch your breath. Were you having any trouble at the time you were first seen in the hospital?
I went to [CITY] and was told I had sepsis from pneumonia. They kept me for a few days, and I briefly felt better after I came home, but it's getting worse every day.
I see you're awake and aware of your surroundings.
You are alert and oriented in all four areas, so your GCS is 15.
I understand this is difficult. On a scale of 0 to 10, how would you rate the intensity of your shortness of breath?
I'd rate it around a 3. I'm able to speak in full sentences now, but it takes a lot of effort to breathe. I also have a cough, but it's productive.
I'm going to check your airway now to ensure it is clear and patent.
Your breathing is labored, and you're grunting on exhale, but your lung sounds are clear. You also have a productive cough with no adventitious sounds.
I'll begin by checking your circulation, including your pulse and skin condition.
I understand. Can you confirm that you have a history of COPD and emphysema, along with some psychiatric conditions like anxiety and depression?
Yes, I struggle with COPD, and I also deal with a lot of anxiety and depression. I recently had an assault and was told I had a broken nose.
I need to take your pulse now. It's currently 126 beats per minute.
Your respiratory rate is 35 breaths per minute, but we will monitor it again after oxygen is administered.
Your blood pressure is 141 over 92. This gives us a starting point.
Your current blood oxygen level is 88% on room air. We are going to administer oxygen now to help improve that.
We will start with a 4-lead EKG now to monitor your heart rhythm.
We'll proceed with a 12-lead EKG for a more detailed heart assessment.
You have strong but very rapid pulses. I'll be watching your heart rate and rhythm closely via continuous monitoring.
I will begin administering oxygen via a nasal cannula at 3 liters per minute to help manage your breathing.
Your oxygen level has improved, and your breathing appears to be a bit easier now. You are rating your shortness of breath at a 3.
I will recheck your respiratory rate now.
Your respiratory rate has improved to 22 breaths per minute.
I will continue to log your blood pressure for comparison as well.
I will attempt to start an IV in your arm to have it ready in case we need to administer any medications.
Your oxygen saturation improved initially to 95%, but it later dropped to 82%. We'll continue monitoring this closely.
I see that you were previously diagnosed with COPD and have a history of emphysema and anxiety. Have you been taking your albuterol regularly since the hospital?
The EKG is showing a pattern of sinus tachycardia. No additional abnormalities like STE or ectopy were found on the 12-lead reading.
You've chosen to be transported to [CITY] as requested. Your condition appears stable for transport.
We're going to move you to the stretcher now. Please keep your legs together and upper body relaxed so we can use a safe and secure technique.
We will closely monitor your vital signs, including oxygen saturation and EKG, as we transport you to the hospital.
How are you feeling so far? Is your breathing still about the same effort?
I'm actually feeling slightly better. Still working hard, but it's somewhat easier now that I have the oxygen.
Great. We will continue to keep an eye on you. The ride should be smooth and you'll be secure throughout.
Is there anything you'd like more or less of to be more comfortable during the ride?
You've had a recent hospitalization and a history of COPD and emphysema. Your follow-up treatment could involve more breathing treatments and antibiotics if needed.
Your vitals continue to remain stable as we approach the hospital.
Are you still rating your breathing at a 3 out of 10?
Yes, it's still around 3. I can talk and take in longer breaths now with less effort.
We're approaching the hospital and will transfer you to the Emergency Department in a secure manner.
All of your medical equipment is ready and will be handed off so the ED will know what to expect.
You'll be handed off into the best care as soon as we arrive. I will personally brief the ED team on your current conditions and history.
A final EKG is on the way and will be in the report for the ED.
We only attempted an IV but were unsuccessful. We are in a stable position and will move forward without it.
We've arrived at the hospital and are being escorted into the ED now.
We are now transferring you to the bed in the ED. You will be monitored continuously.
A complete verbal and written report including your history, vitals, treatments, and EKG findings will be transferred with you.
You are in good hands here. The ED is ready for the next steps.
I will do one final check of your pulse, respiratory rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation before we hand you off.
I'll personally hand the report to the nurse to ensure all your data is included.
Turned off any unnecessary monitoring equipment and removed gear for handoff.
From now on, the care will be handled by the ED team.
The EKG, your medical history, and the vital signs were all passed on to them.
The care steps should be straightforward from here.
The hospital has all they need to continue care. Safe travels ahead.
You're stable and the transport was well-handled.
We'll be heading out now. Have a good day.
I'm signing off for this call. All care was stable and appropriate.
That was a well-managed transport with signs of improvement.
You handled the transport well. Let's see how the ED team proceeds now.
Remember to tell them you had a broken nose from an assault and that you're having trouble affording prescribed medications.
We've noted the broken nose issue and will address financial concerns regarding medications post-stabilization.
All procedures and steps have been completed and documented properly.
Take care now, and I hope everything goes well for you in the hospital.
